Jack Middlemass Scholarship awarded annually.The R. Jack Middlemass Memorial Scholarship was established out of love for Jack and inspired by Jack’s love for baseball and its athletes. In the spirit of Jack’s desire to help others achieve, the fund assists aspiring young people in pursuit of post secondary education. In helping further their scholastic endeavours, the scholarship provides recipients with greater opportunity to excel in life and contribute to the community.Deadline is June 1st.Other Scholarship ResourcesMore information on scholarships is available on the following sites:http://www.collegescholarships.org/https://www.ncaa.org/about/resources/grants-programs-and-scholarshipshttp://www.studentawards.com/Canada Student Grants ProgramProvincial and Territorial Grants and BursariesTrades and apprenticeships grantsScholarshipsCanada.comAssociation of Universities and Colleges of Canada (AUCC)Oberle Scholarship2026 Inaugural Oberle Scholarship(for umpires)The Dean Oberle Memorial Umpire Scholarship was established to honour and celebrate Dean Oberle’s love of sport and his tremendous contributions to officiating. Dean embodied what it means to serve the baseball community with passion, humility, and integrity.This scholarship recognizes an umpire who exemplifies:A genuine love for the sport of baseballA commitment to continuous learning, both independently and with othersA passion for mentorship and helping others growA strong sense of teamwork, collaboration, and community among fellow officialsA consistently positive attitude and uplifting presenceDeadline is June 7, 2026 config.responsive_widget_ids = ["e3f1c304-e9f7-4cac-a4df-7c1ed695ff00","4c72c127-c894-455b-bc46-69ef77204e08","6aba7619-7bdd-4ed8-8833-855183ce8f13"]; Follow usFacebook opens in new windowX (Twitter) opens in new windowInstagram opens in new window Admin Login opens in new windowCopyright © 2026 Ontario Baseball Association

They are chosen by the Ontario Baseball Association Hall of Fame’s Selection Committee.The Ontario Baseball Association Hall of Fame is a virtual Hall of Fame that showcases the Inductees with a picture and biography.Your tour will leave you with a true appreciation for the individuals who have given their all to reach their goals!The deadline for nominations is June 1st annually.Nominate Today2025 Hall of Fame Inductees2025 Hall of Fame InducteesOntario Baseball Association Announces 2025 Hall of Fame InducteesOBA is proud to announce its 2025 Hall of Fame class: former MLB infielder Reno Bertoia, national team standout Tommy McKenzie, renowned historian William Humber, and longtime volunteer Alan Waffle. The induction ceremony will take place October 25 at the Sheraton Toronto Airport Hotel and Conference Centre.Reno Bertoia (1935–2011) played eight seasons with the Detroit Tigers and parts of four others with the Senators and Athletics. Among 271 Canadians to reach MLB, his 612 games rank 26th all-time. After his playing career, he taught in Windsor and scouted for the Tigers and Blue Jays.Tommy McKenzie, 83, represented Canada at five international events, starred in the Intercounty Baseball League with a .315 lifetime average, and later coached multiple championship teams. He is also a member of the Waterloo Region Sports Hall of Fame.William Humber, 75, is Canada’s foremost baseball historian, an Order of Canada recipient, and author of several acclaimed books on the sport. He has been a leading voice in connecting baseball’s roots to Canadian culture and was inducted into the Canadian Baseball Hall of Fame in 2018.Alan Waffle (1947–2025) dedicated nearly four decades to building grassroots baseball in Etobicoke through the Royal York League, which now serves over 1,500 players. A pioneer in expanding opportunities for girls and women, he also played a key role in securing new community facilities.Past Inductee VideosHistory of InducteesHistory of InducteesYear of Induction NameCategory 2025Reno Bertoia2025Tommy McKenzie2025William Humber2025Alan Waffle2024Bill Atkinson2024Jack Carson2024Rob Ducey2024Bob Prentice2024Paul Quantrill2023Frank Colman 2023Greg Hamilton2023Len Andrews2023Rob Thomson2023Troy May2022Marc Picard2022Harvey Bailie2022Gwen Turner2022Don Gilbert2021John Upham2021Jim Ridley 2021Dick Willis 2021Leaside Senior Team 72-89Team2020Fr. Ontario enters the tournament with a roster featuring players from across the province who have earned the opportunity through a series of identification camps, evaluations, and high-performance development programs.2026 Ontario Youth Team RosterCooper DoanMyles StevensBrody BattahLucas SprouleKorbin GreavesAiden MacLellanRyan McIntoshColton LewisBrayden MatreskyLucas HudsonAndrian TeodorowychMatthew BenevidesBrady MartynuikJehmar McClureMason MendesPetro VrantsisKamron NooriNolan CarterCole RoundBrett RienguetteThe selected athletes will continue their preparation throughout the summer before heading to Prince Edward Island to compete for a national championship against the top provincial teams from across Canada.The OBA would like to congratulate all athletes selected to Team Ontario and thank the coaches, families, clubs, and volunteers who have supported their development throughout their baseball journey.Fans can follow Team Ontario's progress throughout the tournament by visiting Baseball Canada and the Ontario Baseball Association's social media channels for updates, scores, and highlights.More NewsNo news articles published.Follow usFacebook opens in new windowX (Twitter) opens in new windowInstagram opens in new window Admin Login opens in new windowCopyright © 2026 Ontario Baseball Association

Jack Middlemass Memorial Scholarship RecipientShareopens in new windowopens in new windowLucas Benham of Waterloo Named 2026 Scholarship WinnerThe Ontario Baseball Association (OBA) is proud to announce Lucas Benham of the Waterloo Tigers 18U AA program as the recipient of the 2026 R. Jack Middlemass Memorial Scholarship.The scholarship is awarded annually to a graduating student-athlete who has demonstrated excellence both on the baseball field and in the classroom, while embodying the values of leadership, sportsmanship, and community involvement that were synonymous with R. Jack Middlemass' lifelong dedication to amateur baseball in Ontario.Lucas has been actively involved in baseball for more than a decade through Waterloo Minor Baseball and currently competes with the Waterloo Tigers 18U program. This fall, he will continue both his academic and athletic careers at Carleton University, where he will study Industrial Design while competing for the Carleton Ravens baseball team.Beyond his achievements on the diamond, Lucas has demonstrated a strong commitment to giving back to his community. He has volunteered with Woolwich Community Services, Waterloo Minor Baseball, the Waterloo Regional Food Bank, and a variety of youth development initiatives. An honour roll student and aspiring coach, Lucas has consistently demonstrated the qualities of leadership, dedication, and service that make him a deserving recipient of this prestigious award.The R. Jack Middlemass Memorial Scholarship was established to honour the legacy of one of Ontario baseball's most respected leaders and supporters. Through this scholarship, the OBA continues to recognize student-athletes who strive for excellence both on and off the field.The Ontario Baseball Association congratulates Lucas Benham on this outstanding achievement and wishes him continued success at Carleton University and beyond.More NewsNo news articles published.Follow usFacebook opens in new windowX (Twitter) opens in new windowInstagram opens in new window Admin Login opens in new windowCopyright © 2026 Ontario Baseball Association

Partnership with BioSteelShareopens in new windowopens in new windowCAMBRIDGE, ON – The Ontario Baseball Association (OBA) is proud to announce the renewal of its partnership with BioSteel, reinforcing a shared commitment to supporting athletes and growing the game of baseball across the province.As the Official Beverage Partner of the OBA, BioSteel will continue to have an on-site presence at Provincial Championships, the OBA Annual General Meeting, and the Best Ever Clinic, helping keep athletes, coaches, officials, and volunteers hydrated with its premium, sugar-free sports hydration products.The renewed partnership reflects a mutual focus on athlete development, performance, and creating positive experiences for players of all ages throughout Ontario."Baseball continues to play a vital role in developing young athletes across Ontario, and it's important for us to support organizations that are creating opportunities for growth both on and off the field," said Dan Crosby, CEO of BioSteel. "The Ontario Baseball Association shares our commitment to athlete development, performance, and community, and we're proud to continue this partnership as we help fuel the next generation with clean, trusted sports nutrition."“Renewing our partnership with BioSteel reflects our shared commitment to athlete health, performance, and the continued growth of baseball across Ontario” said OBA Executive Director, Mary-Ann Smith. “We’re proud to work alongside a partner that aligns so strongly with our values and supports our baseball community.”BioSteel is a leading sports hydration and nutrition brand committed to providing clean, premium products for athletes of all levels. Trusted by athletes, teams, and organizations across North America, BioSteel offers sugar-free hydration and performance solutions formulated to support optimal performance, recovery, and wellness. Many of BioSteel's products are NSF Certified for Sport®, ensuring they meet rigorous standards for quality and safety.The OBA looks forward to another successful season alongside BioSteel as both organizations continue working together to fuel the next generation of baseball players across Ontario.About BioSteel BioSteel is a leading sports hydration and nutrition brand committed to providing clean, premium products for athletes of all levels. Trusted by athletes, teams, and organizations across North America, BioSteel offers sugar-free hydration and performance solutions formulated to support optimal performance, recovery, and wellness. Many of BioSteel's products are NSF Certified for Sport®, ensuring they meet rigorous standardsMore NewsNo news articles published.Follow usFacebook opens in new windowX (Twitter) opens in new windowInstagram opens in new window Admin Login opens in new windowCopyright © 2026 Ontario Baseball Association

Create Positive Sport 2026 Best Ever Clinic BEC Drill of the Week PCCP - Provincial Coach Certification SAFE SPORT keyboard_arrow_down Safesport - File a Report OBA Safe Sport Framework keyboard_arrow_rightOBA Safe Sport Framework Safe Sport Awareness Portal Sanctions Rowan's Law Safety Insurance Mental Health in Sport Rules keyboard_arrow_down 2025 AGM (Jan 2026) By-Laws & Playing Rules Rules of Baseball Approved Baseballs Bat Rules Rep Rules Committee Convention 2025 Starting Line Up Applications & Forms keyboard_arrow_down Rep keyboard_arrow_right Rep Sanctioned Tournament Hosting Application Rep Provincial Tournament Hosting Application Select keyboard_arrow_right Select Sanctioned Tournament Hosting Application Select Provincial Tournament Hosting Application National Qualifiers keyboard_arrow_right National Qualifiers Entry Application Coach Clinic Hosting Application Umpire Camp Hosting Application Grassroots keyboard_arrow_right Baseball5 Rally Cap MORE keyboard_arrow_down CONTACT keyboard_arrow_right Board of Directors Rep Council Select Council Office Staff Contact OBA 2026 OBA Partners Inside Pitch Grassroots Programs keyboard_arrow_right Baseball5 Rally Cap Challenger Baseball opens in new window ONCA Information OBA Policies Calendar Scholarships Hall of Fame Photos Baseball Ontario Clothing OBA Partners with Gummy Gainz as Official Snack PartnerShareopens in new windowopens in new windowBecome the official snack for the 2026 & 2027 seasonsCAMBRIDGE, ON – The Ontario Baseball Association (OBA) is proud to announce a new partnership with Gummy Gainz, a leading Canadian snack company and supporter of community-based sports.Under the agreement, Gummy Gainz becomes the Official Snack Partner of the OBA. The partnership will see Gummy Gainz provide on-site activation at local and provincial tournaments, the OBA Annual General Meeting, and the Best Ever Clinic. Through this collaboration, athletes of all ages across Ontario will have access to Gummy Gainz’s healthy, protein-packed snacks."The Ontario Baseball Association is thrilled to welcome Gummy Gainz as our Official Snack Partner for the 2026 and 2027 seasons," said OBA Executive Director, Mary-Ann Smith."Their innovative, protein-packed snacks align perfectly with our mission of supporting healthy, high-energy athletes across the province. Together, we’re bringing a fun, functional, and exciting way to fuel players, coaches, and fans. We look forward to an incredible partnership that enhances the baseball experience across Ontario.""Gummy Gainz was born to prove that fueling your performance doesn't have to be boring," said Ben Poss, Co-Founder of Gummy Gainz. "Partnering with the OBA allows us to put a better-for-you option directly into the hands of Ontario's next generation of baseball players - a product they'll actually want to reach for. We're proud to support an association dedicated to developing athletes and elevating the game of baseball across Canada."Gummy Gainz is a functional candy brand focused on making protein more enjoyable, approachable, and accessible for active lifestyles. The company was founded after a simple question:Why does healthy candy have to taste bad?What began as a conversation about chalky tasting bars, hard-to-hit protein goals, and "healthy" treats that failed to satisfy quickly turned into a mission. In 2023, the founders of Gummy Gainz went back to the kitchen and got to work.After months of recipe development, testing, and taste trials, they cracked the code: real candy taste packed with 11 grams of protein in every bag.By mid-2024, Gummy Gainz launched from its own candy lab in Vancouver. Today, the brand can be found in more than 500 retail locations and continues to grow while staying true to its original mission - creating snacks that are functional, flavourful, and genuinely enjoyable.Each bag delivers delicious flavour, protein power, and zero compromise. Whether you're fueling up between games, recovering after a workout, or simply craving a better snack option, Gummy Gainz offers a snack you can feel good about.About the Ontario Baseball AssociationThe Ontario Baseball Association is the governing body for amateur baseball in Ontario, serving more than 100,000 athletes, coaches, officials, and volunteers. The OBA is committed to providing opportunities for participation, development, and excellence in baseball across the province.About Gummy GainzGummy Gainz is a Canadian functional candy company dedicated to making protein fun. By combining the taste of real candy with meaningful nutritional benefits, Gummy Gainz is redefining what a protein snack can be for athletes and active individuals alike.Find them in 500+ stores and growing, along with baseball tournaments throughout Ontario and select concession stand locations.Each bag delivers delicious flavour, protein power, and zero compromise. Whether you're fueling up between games, recovering after a workout, or simply craving a better snack option, Gummy Gainz offers a snack you can feel good about.Visit Gummy Gainz online at gummygainz.ca and follow them @gotgummygainzMore NewsNo news articles published.Follow usFacebook opens in new windowX (Twitter) opens in new windowInstagram opens in new window Admin Login opens in new windowCopyright © 2026 Ontario Baseball Association
headings: OBA Partners with Gummy Gainz as Official Snack Partner

'Less' : 'More'}}keyboard_arrow_down {{error.field}} - {{error.message}} GuidelinesPlease ensure that all participating teams are aware of the following:The team has permission from its association to play in your tournamentLocal Association has insurance with a Certificate of Insurance issued by OBAOR for out-of-province teams, the team has proof of insurance in the form of a Certificate of Insurance. (If needed, you may ask that your tournament and facilities be added as additional insured) The Following Rules are Mandatory:OBA Arm Care (excludes use of Pitch Count App)Rowan’s LawNo Metal Cleats Select Bat Rules Uniform & Equipment Rules (including helmets) as shown belowUniforms & Equipment 3.1 All players and coaches will be dressed in a baseball team uniform as issued by their home association; minimum uniform being long pants, an association-issued shirt with a number and a cap. No sandals, open-toed shoes or shorts are permitted on the diamond. Coaches may wear golf shirts without numbers or similar, if that is the “team issued” coach attire but numbered shirts are preferred. 3.2 All catchers are required to wear a mask with throat protector, helmet, chest protector, shin guards, a cup / pelvic protector during the game and warm-ups in all divisions. 3.3 Anyone warming up a pitcher must wear a mask & throat protector or full helmet. Standing up or crouched down requires a mask or full helmet. 3.4 All players must wear a protective athletic support / pelvic protector.3.5 The on-deck batter, batter, base runners, and batboy/batgirl must wear a prescribed protective helmet with flaps, covering both ears. This may not be changed except after the inning has been completed, or the player has been retired or has crossed home plate. Helmet chinstraps are mandatory unless the presence of a manufacturer installed face guard (C‐Flap) prevents the use of the chinstrap and will be worn as designed by the manufacturer. 3.6 Bats approved by Baseball Canada for the individual age groups are allowed. 3.7 9U pitchers must wear an approved batting helmet with a facemask. 3.8 9U coach operating the pitching machine must wear a baseball helmet, which can be a skull cap.If you have any questions, please contact OBA at Tournaments@playoba.caAge Division8U9U10U11U12U13U14U15U16U18U22ULocal Association NameChair PersonFirst NameLast NamePhoneEmailTournament Start DateTournament End DateEntry FeeNumber of TeamsSpecial NotesPaymentDiscountSubtotalTaxTotal USDSubmitThe form has been submitted. config.responsive_widget_ids = ["a53664b7-85c2-4d35-88eb-4311b7a13ab6"]; Follow usFacebook opens in new windowX (Twitter) opens in new windowInstagram opens in new window Admin Login opens in new windowCopyright © 2026 Ontario Baseball Association

CommitteeRequesting an Ontario Baseball association Rep Division Release Appeal for the 2026 Season:The OBA Rep Division Appeal Form must be completed in full and submitted with all supporting documentation.2026 Application for Rep Division Release AppealAs per c) in Section RP1.6 of the OBA Rep Division Procedures and Playing Rules:"The appeal must include all evidence and correspondence submitted to the Local Association and/or the Affiliated Association and copies of all decisions made by the Local Association and Affiliated Association."Upon receipt and review of all required information, the release can either be granted by the Technical Chair or they may determine that a Technical Release Committee Hearing should be scheduled.Note: A Baseball Ontario Technical Committee Hearing will not be organized until all necessary documentation is provided.NewsOBA Announces 2026 Ontario Youth Team Roster for Baseball Canada CupOBA Announces 2026 R. Select games are optional and are in addition to house league commitments.Dedicated Select (DS) Requires all players to be registered and play with the local association in the house league division and/or the select division. Players are not required to play in the house league division. Select Membership Must be a member of the Select Division. This also includes Little League franchises.Must be a member of the Select Division. This also includes Little League franchises.Rosters Each team must have an exclusive roster. Rosters must be submitted through the Baseball Ontario Website and include HS at the beginning of their team namesEach team must have an exclusive roster. Rosters must be submitted through the Baseball Ontario Website and include DS at the beginning of their team namesCoaching Please refer to: 2024 Coach CertificationNote* If you are a select team playing in a rep tournament you must have the requirements of a Rep AA and below coachPlease refer to: 2024 Coach CertificationNote* If you are a select team playing in a rep tournament you must have the requirements of a Rep AA and below coach Insurance All players must be insured in the Select Section of the Baseball Ontario insurance applicationAll players must be insured in the Select Section of the Baseball Ontario insurance applicationPractices 1-2 events a week coupled with games 1-2 events a week coupled with gamesGames 1-2 events a week coupled with practices 1-2 events a week coupled with practicesTournaments Potential of fewer tournaments or tournaments may be closer to homeTournament hosts will identify what teams can applyPotential of fewer tournaments or tournaments may be closer to homeTournament hosts will identify what teams can applyProvincials Exclusive House League Select ProvincialsExclusive Dedicated Select ProvincialsSelect Loops Granted there is a sufficient number of teams, select loops may operate where only HS teams play each otherGranted there is a sufficient number of teams, select loops may operate where only DS teams play each otherTeam Selection Often teams are chosen in the springOften teams are chosen in the fallCommitment More flexible commitmentHigher commitmentAge Division May have a team of the same age division in Dedicated SelectMay have a team of the same age division in House League SelectCost Determined by the local associationDetermined by the local associationRule Changes There are no different playing rules for House League Select. All Select Rules can be found here: OnDeck Select RulesThere are no different playing rules for House League Select. Create Positive Sport 2026 Best Ever Clinic BEC Drill of the Week PCCP - Provincial Coach Certification SAFE SPORT keyboard_arrow_down Safesport - File a Report OBA Safe Sport Framework keyboard_arrow_rightOBA Safe Sport Framework Safe Sport Awareness Portal Sanctions Rowan's Law Safety Insurance Mental Health in Sport Rules keyboard_arrow_down 2025 AGM (Jan 2026) By-Laws & Playing Rules Rules of Baseball Approved Baseballs Bat Rules Rep Rules Committee Convention 2025 Starting Line Up Applications & Forms keyboard_arrow_down Rep keyboard_arrow_right Rep Sanctioned Tournament Hosting Application Rep Provincial Tournament Hosting Application Select keyboard_arrow_right Select Sanctioned Tournament Hosting Application Select Provincial Tournament Hosting Application National Qualifiers keyboard_arrow_right National Qualifiers Entry Application Coach Clinic Hosting Application Umpire Camp Hosting Application Grassroots keyboard_arrow_right Baseball5 Rally Cap MORE keyboard_arrow_down CONTACT keyboard_arrow_right Board of Directors Rep Council Select Council Office Staff Contact OBA 2026 OBA Partners Inside Pitch Grassroots Programs keyboard_arrow_right Baseball5 Rally Cap Challenger Baseball opens in new window ONCA Information OBA Policies Calendar Scholarships Hall of Fame Photos Baseball Ontario Clothing Recognition PolicyRecognition PolicyBaseball Ontario Recognition PolicyUpdated October 27, 2024Rep Council shall automatically recognize any baseball program operating in Ontario which:submits to Baseball Ontario an application in the form approved by Rep Council;pays the fees established by Rep Council; andoperates only at the 13U to 18U levels, with no 11U or younger teams.Recognized programs will be required to provide to Baseball Ontario a roster of the players participating on each of the program’s teams. The recognized program will be required to update the roster filed with Baseball Ontario as changes are made to the roster.Recognized programs will be required to purchase insurance through Baseball Ontario at the member rate, or provide evidence of satisfactory liability insurance coverage.A recognized program that is found to be operating, directly or indirectly, a 11U or younger team will automatically and immediately have its recognition suspended and will become a non-recognized program.Subject to the foregoing clauses, recognition shall be effective from August 1st to July 31st annually, or the date application is received until July 31st.A team of all 14 year olds will be considered a 15U team. A team with any players 13 years of age or younger will be considered a 13U team or younger team.So long as a program is recognized, the following shall apply:teams from the recognized program may play OBA rep teams or other OBA recognized teams with no penalties or sanctions for either team;teams from the recognized program may play in OBA elite tournaments (if the tournament host accepts the team’s application to the tournament);teams from the recognized program will not be eligible for OBA Rep National Qualifiers or OBA Rep Play-offs, unless the team complies with all residency requirements of P1 (i.e. all required release are obtained, the team registers with and is approved by an Affiliated Association, etc.);players from the recognized program will be eligible to also register with an OBA rep team in compliance with section P1.12 e).players from the recognized program are eligible to try out for the Ontario Youth Team operated by Baseball Ontario; andplayers from the recognized program will be considered to be registered with Baseball Ontario for purposes of eligibility for the Canadian Junior National Team.Nothing in this Recognition Proposal takes any existing rights away from the Premier Baseball League of Ontario (“PBLO”) or any PBLO team set out in the agreement between Baseball Ontario and the PBLO so long as that agreement remains in existence. Recognition of the PBLO is mandated in clause P1.12 d) i) 5).In addition to programs which apply to Baseball Ontario, Rep Council may determine to recognize other groups at the request of an Affiliated Association, including:for the purposes of P1.12 a), Ontario Senior Baseball Association, and all Senior Men’s house league programs; andfor the purposes of P1.12 b), AABC, Can-Am (Windsor area senior league), American Legion, NABF, Little League and USSSA.DOWNLOAD THE POLICY2026 RECOGNITION APPLICATIONDownload the Media Release from July 24, 2017 config.responsive_widget_ids = ["aa8ea037-dfd3-4f23-8c1f-dc04c62cf000"]; Follow usFacebook opens in new windowX (Twitter) opens in new windowInstagram opens in new window Admin Login opens in new windowCopyright © 2026 Ontario Baseball Association

We AgreeWe confirm that our organization will not operate any 11U or younger teams. By completing this application, we acknowledge that the organization automatically suspends its recognition, if it operates, directly or indirectly, a 11U or younger team at any time. We understand that a suspension of our recognition does not entitle us to a refund of our fees paid for recognition.Acknowledgement of Fees We agreeThe organization shall include the following fees with the filing of this application: (a) If the organization was not recognized by Baseball Ontario for the 2025 season, payment of $1,000 as a one-time fee; and(b) Annually, a fee of $300/team for each team that the organization will operate for the 2026 seasonWe acknowledge that we will not be recognized until our fees are paid, including any outstanding fees from any prior year.Documents to be provided We agreeAs part of recognition, the organization shall deliver to Baseball Ontario prior to commencing its 2026 season the following documents:1. A roster for each team which the organization is operating for the 2026 season, which roster shall be updated to reflect any changes; and2. title: OBA Celebrates Record Turnout at Royal York Girls Umpire Camp - Ontario Baseball Association
snippet: Skip to contentopens in new windowOntario Baseball Associationmenu TOURNAMENTS keyboard_arrow_down Rep Tournaments keyboard_arrow_right 2026 Ontario Summer Games 2026 Rep Tournaments opens in new window Tournament Host Resources keyboard_arrow_rightTournament

Create Positive Sport 2026 Best Ever Clinic BEC Drill of the Week PCCP - Provincial Coach Certification SAFE SPORT keyboard_arrow_down Safesport - File a Report OBA Safe Sport Framework keyboard_arrow_rightOBA Safe Sport Framework Safe Sport Awareness Portal Sanctions Rowan's Law Safety Insurance Mental Health in Sport Rules keyboard_arrow_down 2025 AGM (Jan 2026) By-Laws & Playing Rules Rules of Baseball Approved Baseballs Bat Rules Rep Rules Committee Convention 2025 Starting Line Up Applications & Forms keyboard_arrow_down Rep keyboard_arrow_right Rep Sanctioned Tournament Hosting Application Rep Provincial Tournament Hosting Application Select keyboard_arrow_right Select Sanctioned Tournament Hosting Application Select Provincial Tournament Hosting Application National Qualifiers keyboard_arrow_right National Qualifiers Entry Application Coach Clinic Hosting Application Umpire Camp Hosting Application Grassroots keyboard_arrow_right Baseball5 Rally Cap MORE keyboard_arrow_down CONTACT keyboard_arrow_right Board of Directors Rep Council Select Council Office Staff Contact OBA 2026 OBA Partners Inside Pitch Grassroots Programs keyboard_arrow_right Baseball5 Rally Cap Challenger Baseball opens in new window ONCA Information OBA Policies Calendar Scholarships Hall of Fame Photos Baseball Ontario Clothing OBA Celebrates Record Turnout at Royal York Girls Umpire CampShareopens in new windowopens in new windowThe Ontario Baseball Association (OBA) is proud to celebrate the outstanding success of the Royal York Girls Umpire Camp, which saw an incredible turnout and growing momentum for women in officiating across the province.A total of 44 pre-registered participants, along with 4 walk-ins, brought the camp to an impressive 48 female umpires in attendance. Including instructors, the event welcomed 50 women on the field — a powerful and inspiring milestone for the continued growth of female participation in baseball umpiring.The camp delivered a high-energy and engaging experience, with participants showcasing enthusiasm, skill development, and a strong commitment to the game. Attendees received valuable information on future opportunities within Ontario, including pathways through the OBA Women’s Umpire Network, mentorship calls, the Ontario Women’s Invitational (OMG Tournament), and the Ontario Summer Games.Feedback from both participants and attending families was overwhelmingly positive, highlighting the importance of accessible, inclusive programming and the impact of creating dedicated spaces for women in baseball.The Ontario Baseball Association remains committed to growing the game and providing meaningful opportunities for women and girls at all levels.For more information on umpire development and how to get involved, please visit www.playoba.ca.More NewsNo news articles published.Follow usFacebook opens in new windowX (Twitter) opens in new windowInstagram opens in new window Admin Login opens in new windowCopyright © 2026 Ontario Baseball Association
headings: OBA Celebrates Record Turnout at Royal York Girls Umpire Camp

'Less' : 'More'}}keyboard_arrow_down {{error.field}} - {{error.message}} Baseball Ontario is pleased to share that the first two levels of PCCP training are now available! The PCCP program is designed to help first-time coaches implement the fundamentals of baseball, which are broken down by age.Modules cover Baserunning, Catching, Hitting, Infielding, Outfielding, and Throwing.We have developed a seasonal subscription program locals can opt to purchase on behalf of their coaches. The subscription would give the local association access to the certification modules for their entire house league, as well as Select or Rep Teams at the 8U/9U level and below.Subscription Options:# of Teams in your Organization*Fee0-10:$100.0011-30:$250.0031-50:$500.0051+:$750.00* This is the total number of House League teams plus the number of Select or Rep Teams at the 8/9U AA Level and below, in your organization.IE: Local Association has 28 house league teams, 2 9U select teams and 4 9U rep teams, total # of teams is 34, so your subscription level is $500.Alternatively, coaches can purchase the PCCP certification on their own at a cost of $40 for 2026, which includes both 7U and 9U PCCP modules currently, and 11U and 13U coming soon. This would be done in the OnDeck app.Local Association NameFull name please, no acronymsContact NameFirst NameLast NameThe individual who will manage the group subscriptionEmailPCCP SubscriptionSelect0-10 Teams - $100.0011-30 Teams - $250.0031-50 Teams - $500.0051+ Teams - $750.00Payment Info:Payment Methods:E-Transfer sent to epayments@baseballontario.com (choose "submit now and pay later")Cheque (choose "submit now and pay later") Please note, a 10% admin fee will be charged for cheque payments.Once payment is received, Baseball Ontario will issue your association a voucher that can be redeemed by your coaches through our OnDeck Platform, where they will create a profile and register for the PCCP courses!PaymentDiscountSubtotalTaxTotal CAD Submit now and pay laterSubmitTHANK YOU FOR SUBMITTING YOUR request for a local association pccp subscription.PAYMENT OPTIONS:E-transfer to epayments@playoba.ca (please ensure you include the name of the association in the e-transfer message)Cheque (Payable to Baseball Ontario and mailed to the office at the address below)Baseball Ontario6-425 Hespeler Road, Suite 104Cambridge, ON N1R8J6 config.responsive_widget_ids = ["49dc9ceb-4303-45c8-afdb-c0722ccf268a"]; Follow usFacebook opens in new windowX (Twitter) opens in new windowInstagram opens in new window Admin Login opens in new windowCopyright © 2026 Ontario Baseball Association
headings: THANK YOU FOR SUBMITTING YOUR request for a local association pccp subscription.

Player1. The player must have been born in Ontario or resided in the province during the period of outstanding performance over a lengthy period. Any such accomplishment must have included a playing career in an Ontario Baseball Association program.PLAYER NOMINATIONBuilder2. A builder must have been born in Ontario or have resided in the province during the period of outstanding contribution and must have demonstrated exceptional service to baseball over a lengthy period. (Builder includes officials, writers, broadcasters, and executives.)Builder NominationTeams3. Teams representing a particular community or province with distinction will be considered. The recognition of such teams must exceed more than one year.Team NominationSpecial Category4. Nominations for the “Special” category can include teams of a single year’s accomplishments and groups of players or builders who have made a significant contribution.Special Category NominationThe Ontario Baseball Association Hall of Fame Committee shall make the final decision on what information received by the Nominator, Supporters and or Inductee will be used at the Induction Ceremony. config.responsive_widget_ids = ["59cbb636-f9cf-4f98-855a-ad4fe2997e79","6459ac08-a645-4a1c-b37d-b4d301471248","d919f4ac-d511-40dd-a1c0-e3e8c1a74dc5"]; Follow usFacebook opens in new windowX (Twitter) opens in new windowInstagram opens in new window Admin Login opens in new windowCopyright © 2026 Ontario Baseball Association

BiosPitching Clinic - BiosKate PsotaJohn OgiltreeKate Psota is one of Canada’s most decorated female baseball players, and is being inducted into the Canadian Baseball Hall of Fame with the class of 2026!By the time Kate was 15 she focused on baseball, playing on multiple teams. She played Boys AA baseball before joining the newly formed women’s program. She started to play provincial programs when she was 16 and first represented Canada in a tournament in Toronto in 2001.Kate’s baseball achievements are impressive being recognized as one of the greats of Canadian Women’s baseball. Kate was only 13 when she was named to Team Ontario in 1999. Representing Ontario Kate was a nine-time national champion, all while earning fame and success for Canada on the world stage. She was the first female to be honoured as Player of the Year by Baseball Ontario in 2002.She joined the newly formed national team when she was 18 and has represented Canada in every IBAF (International Baseball Federation) World Cup since 2004. Kate has earned many personal and team accolades along the way. She has helped Canada win 6 World Cup medals ( 2 silver and four bronze) and was named to the World Cup All Star team at first base in 2010, 2012, 2016, and 2018.In 2008 Kate earned the Jimmy Rattlesnake Award from Baseball Canada for her on-field accomplishments as well as team spirit and leadership and was named Canada Baseball National MVP in 2009 and 2010.Garnering respect and admiration on the baseball field for Canada, Kate also pursued hockey at the post-secondary level, playing 5 years at Wilfrid Laurier University. At WLU Kate helped the team win five consecutive OUA championships from 2006 to 2010, earning a CIS (Now USports) bronze medal in 2010.Kate accomplished in the classroom as well, gaining Academic All Canadian honours in 2009. She loved playing hockey, but it was baseball that was her true passion. Kate helped Canada win a silver medal at the 2015 Pan American Games in Toronto. Her last game for Canada was an exciting extra-innings game against the United States to win the bronze medal at the 2018 World Cup.A true trail blazer for the Women’s game, and a role model for the next generation of world class baseball players, Kate was only 18 when she played in her first World Cup and now is giving back to the game as a member of the coaching staff for Canada at next summer’s World Cup in Thunder Bay.John Ogiltree is a Canadian former baseball pitcher. Born in Toronto, he is a graduate of Martin Methodist College.At the age of 18 he was originally taken in the June 1996 Regular Draft in the 32nd round (941st overall) by Detroit Tigers but decided to attend college instead.In 2000, he debuted for Team Canada, in the qualifiers for the 2001 Baseball World Cup. The tall right-hander, at 6' 8", was 7–3 with a 2.30 ERA and 97 K through 74 1/3 IP as a senior in 2001, making honorable mention on the SLAM! Canadian Baseball All-Canadian team.He was signed by their chief Canadian scout, the late Jim Ridley, for the Toronto Blue Jays as an undrafted free agent after his senior year of college.With the Toronto Blue Jays he played first with the Medicine Hat Blue Jays in 2001, the Dunedin Blue Jays in 2002, the New Haven Ravens in 2003, the New Hampshire Fisher Cats in both 2004 and 2005 before moving to the Washington Nationals organization where he pitched briefly with the Potomac Nationals and Harrisburg Senators in 2005.He was with Team Canada in the qualifiers for the 2004 Summer Olympics, as they locked up one of two American spots (alongside Cuba) in Athens, which finished in fourth place.Following his professional playing days, Ogiltree returned to Canada to play in the Intercounty Baseball League (IBL), playing with the Brantford Red Sox from 2006 to 2009.Prior to going pro, he also spent some time in the IBL, playing with the Guelph Royals from 1997 to 2000.Overall, he finished his minor league career with a record of 19–19 with 39 saves and a 3.62 ERA.His sister Tamara Ogiltree is also a very gifted ball player.In June 2007 John married Elizabeth Tracz. Elizabeth is graduate of Providence College. config.responsive_widget_ids = ["a9145171-94ff-4b67-8d30-22236a497743"]; Follow usFacebook opens in new windowX (Twitter) opens in new windowInstagram opens in new window Admin Login opens in new windowCopyright © 2026 Ontario Baseball Association

'Less' : 'More'}}keyboard_arrow_down {{error.field}} - {{error.message}} CONCUSSION AWARENESSUnder Rowan's Law, before any player can be registered for the 16U Provincial Team tryouts, the player, and the parent or legal guardian of the player if the player is under 18 years of age, must review one of the Concussion Education Resources provided by the Province of Ontario and must review the Player Code of Conduct. Links to these resources are provided here and will open in a separate window:PROVINCIAL OF ONTARIO CONCUSSION EDUCATION RESOURCES:www.ontario.ca/page/rowans-law-concussion-safetyBASEBALL ONTARIO PLAYER CODE OF CONDUCT:Player Code of ConductI hereby confirm that: (1) the player being registered to participate in baseball activities with Baseball Ontario, and the parent or legal guardian of the player being registered if the player is under 18 years of age, have reviewed one of the concussion education resources provided by the Province of Ontario as referenced above and (2) agree to comply with the player code of conduct(s) as referenced above. I / we further acknowledge understanding the nature and risk of concussion and head injury to athletes, including the risks of continuing to play after a concussion or head injury is suspected.Confirmation of Concussion Awareness I hereby provide my confirmation of Concussion Awareness as outlined above.PLAYER INFORMATION:NameFirst NameLast NameEmailPhoneAddressStreet AddressCityCountryDate of BirthMust be born in 2010 or laterBatsLeftRightSwitchThrowsLeftRightAmbidextrousPrimary PositionPitcherCatcher1st Base2nd Base3rd BaseShort StopInfieldLeft FieldCentre FieldRight FieldOutfieldSecondary PositionPitcherCatcher1st Base2nd Base3rd BaseShort StopInfieldLeft FieldCentre FieldRight FieldOutfieldJersey Size (Men's Sizing)XSSMLXLXXLFitted Hat SizeTryout you wish to attend:June 13, 1-4pmJune 21, 8:45-11:15amPARENT/GUARDIAN INFORMATIONNameFirst NameLast NameEmailPhonePARENTAL CONSENT:All players must have permission from their parent/legal guardian in order to register and participate in the 16U Provincial Team Tryouts. In addition, by confirming consent, the parent/legal guardian acknowledges that Baseball Ontario may publish general information about players including, but not limited to, the player's name, playing statistics, photograph(s) etc., as this information relates to the 16U Provincial Team Tryouts and the National 16U Tournament. By checking the box below, the parent/legal guardian confirms that he/she has read all the information provided regarding the 16U Provincial Team Tryouts (including the Baseball Ontario waivers) and agrees with same.Confirmation of Parental Consent I hereby provide my confirmation of Parental Consent as outlined above.PAYMENT INFO:The tryout fee is $45, payable by:PayPal (choose "pay now" below to be redirected to PayPal)E-Transfer (choose "submit now and pay later")Please note, if you choose PayPal and click "pay now" but do not finish the transaction, we will not receive the registration and it will need to be re-submitted.Details for e-transfer will be included in the confirmation email that will be sent to the email address provided for the parent/guardian.16U Tryout FeePrice$45.00PaymentDiscountSubtotalTaxTotal CAD Submit now and pay later Pay nowSubmitTHANK YOU FOR SUBMITTING YOUR 2025 16U provincial team TRYOUTS APPLICATION.PAYMENT OPTION (IF YOU DIDN'T COMPLETE THE PAYPAL PAYMENT):E-transfer to joe@playoba.ca (please ensure you include the player's name in the e-transfer message) config.responsive_widget_ids = ["84a9f17a-305c-4fda-a21a-30c7149a3bfb","0f04a835-5926-4c61-894e-9adb6c42bdc9","412130d0-6261-420c-b06b-de4c2ca73242"]; Follow usFacebook opens in new windowX (Twitter) opens in new windowInstagram opens in new window Admin Login opens in new windowCopyright © 2026 Ontario Baseball Association

title: Arm Care Pitch Count Rules Faq
snippet: Skip to main content Arm Care & Pitch Count Rules FAQ – Arm Care COACHING CENTRE UMPIRE ZONE FEMALE BASEBALL ARM CARE MORE SIGN IN / REGISTER Arm Care & Pitch Count Rules FAQ Updated for the 2026 baseball season Arm Care & Pitch Count Rule FAQ "How do I..." -
searchText: Arm Care Pitch Count Rules Faq Arm Care & Pitch Count Rules FAQ Arm Care & Pitch Count Rule FAQ Skip to main content Arm Care & Pitch Count Rules FAQ – Arm Care COACHING CENTRE UMPIRE ZONE FEMALE BASEBALL ARM CARE MORE SIGN IN / REGISTER Arm Care & Pitch Count Rules FAQ Updated for the 2026 baseball season Arm Care & Pitch Count Rule FAQ "How do I..." - A full guide to the Pitch Count App Where can I find information on the Arm Care rules? What are the pitch count limits and days rest requirements for each division? What happens when a pitcher reaches their limit? Can pitchers pitch on multiple games on the same day? Can pitchers pitch two days in a row? Can pitchers pitch three days in a row? Can a player pitch and catch in the same game/day? Who is responsible for entering the pitch count and when? Do pitch counts have to be entered live in-game, or can they be entered after the game? What if I don't have Wi-Fi or data at the park? What should the home team do if either team has reached their pitch count limit? What are my obligations when traveling outside of Ontario or Canada? What penalties apply when a team violates the pitch count rules? What happens when a team is not in the app? What happens when a player is not in the app? There's an error on my roster in the app, how can I fix it? How do I submit a trouble ticket for the Pitch Count app? "HOW DO I...?" A FULL GUIDE TO THE PITCH COUNT APP Have you ever wondered how to do something on the Pitch Count app? OBA has created a full series of How-To videos and Frequently Asked Questions on using the Pitch Count App. Please visit coachoba.ca for all the how-to and FAQ you will need! [TOP] WHERE CAN I FIND INFORMATION ON THE ARM CARE RULES? The full Arm Care rules can be found in the Baseball Ontario ONDeck app in the OBA Procedures and Playing Rules under Section A12. OBA Playing Rules [TOP] WHAT ARE THE PITCH COUNT LIMITS AND DAYS REST REQUIREMENTS FOR EACH DIVISION? Day 1 of the rest period is the day after the game the pitcher last pitched. Days rest is calculated based on the total number of pitches thrown. For example, if an 11U pitcher throws 65 pitches on Friday, their days rest would be 3-days and they would be permitted to next pitch again on Tuesday. "Last Batter" can be declared prior to the threshold being reached and any pitches thrown after the threshold are not counted. Example: The 15U threshold for zero days rest is 35 pitches. The pitcher begins the new batter at 32 pitches. Prior to the 35th pitch, the Manager indicates that this shall be the last batter for the pitcher. The pitcher is permitted to complete the current batter and a final pitch count of 35 is recorded. [TOP] WHAT HAPPENS WHEN A PITCHER REACHES THEIR LIMIT? When a pitcher reaches their pitch count limit during a game, and they are in the middle of an at-bat, the pitcher is permitted to continue to pitch until that batter is retired (under most circumstances). Please see Can Pitchers Pitch Three Days in a Row? below for the exception to this rule. When a pitcher reaches the maximum number of pitches allowed for any days-rest threshold, they may complete the at-bat without affecting their days rest, as long as they do not pitch to another batter. In this situation, the number of pitches corresponding to the threshold will be indicated on the pitching log (not the actual pitch count). The coach or manager must acknowledge this with the umpire prior to the threshold pitch being thrown. The umpire would then notify the official scorekeeper. Example: The 15U threshold for zero days rest is 35 pitches. The pitcher begins the new batter at 32 pitches. Prior to the 35th pitch, the Manager indicates that this shall be the last batter for the pitcher. The pitcher is permitted to complete the current batter and a final pitch count of 35 is recorded. At the end of the at-bat, the pitcher must be removed from the pitching position. [TOP] CAN PITCHERS PITCH IN MULTIPLE GAMES ON THE SAME DAY? Yes, pitchers can pitch in 2 games (max) on the same day as long as they don’t exceed their no rest required threshold in the first game of the day. The pitch count for both games will be added together to calculate days rest required. For example, a 15U pitcher could throw 25 pitches in game 1 of a doubleheader and in game 2 can pitch again because they haven’t exceeded their no-days rest threshold. They start game 2 at pitch 26 and can throw a total of 95 pitches combined over the two games. [TOP] CAN PITCHERS PITCH TWO DAYS IN A ROW? Yes, pitchers can pitch on two consecutive days as long as they haven’t exceeded their no rest required threshold on the first day and have only pitched in one game on the first day. On Day 2, the pitcher starts the day with the same number of pitches thrown on Day 1. So, for example, if a pitcher threw 20 pitches on Friday, they can pitch again on Saturday but their pitch count for the day starts at 21, not at zero. Pitchers can not pitch in more than 2 games in any 2-day period. In all cases, the pitch count for all games are added together to calculate days rest required. [TOP] CAN PITCHERS PITCH THREE DAYS IN A ROW? Yes, pitchers can pitch on three consecutive days as long as they do not exceed their no rest required threshold in all three games combined. For example, at 13U, a pitcher could throw 10 pitches each day on Friday, Saturday, and Sunday; as long as the pitcher does not exceed 30 pitches over the three consecutive days, this is permitted. If a pitcher throws on three consecutive days, they must rest for 1 day before they are eligible to pitch again. [TOP] CAN A PLAYER PITCH AND CATCH IN THE SAME GAME/DAY? No. Players can not pitch and catch in the same day regardless of which position they started in. Once a player has assumed the pitching position, they are no longer permitted to catch for the remainder of the day. Once a player has assumed the catching position, they are no longer permitted to pitch for the remainder of the day. [TOP] WHO IS RESPONSIBLE FOR ENTERING THE PITCH COUNT? The Home Team is always responsible for entering the pitch count for both teams. The Head Coach is accountable for the pitch count and responsible to ensure that it is entered. However, the coach may delegate the pitch count to a trusted assistant coach, team manager, trainer, or other volunteer. [TOP] WHEN DO PITCH COUNTS HAVE TO BE ENTERED? DO PITCH COUNTS HAVE TO BE ENTERED LIVE IN-GAME OR CAN THEY BE ENTERED AFTER THE GAME? In Rep and Select tournaments, pitch counts must be entered in the app in real-time during the game (either pitch-by-pitch or at the end of each half-inning). During regular season play it is permitted to enter pitch counts after the game as long as they are entered within 24 hours. However, OBA strongly recommends pitch-by-pitch tracking in the app for all games to ensure that coaches are notified of potential violations before they occur to avoid penalties to their team. Pitch-by-pitch tracking also displays real-time pitch counts, pitches remaining, and dramatically reduces the volume of data entry errors versus entering pitch counts post-game. [TOP] WHAT IF I DON'T HAVE ACCESS TO WI-FI OR DATA AT THE PARK? The Pitch Count app works without Wi-Fi or mobile data. Simply track your pitches as you normally would and submit the final pitch count as soon as you reconnect to Wi-Fi or data. The app uses minimal data since it's not transmitting large amounts of information. [TOP] WHAT SHOULD THE HOME TEAM DO IF EITHER TEAM HAS REACHED THEIR PITCH COUNT LIMIT? If pitch counts are being recorded pitch-by-pitch, the pitch counter will receive alerts, the screen will turn ORANGE on their device when a player is approaching and has reached their maximum pitch count. As it is the Home team's responsibility to capture the count, it is also the responsibility for the Home team's Manager/Head Coach to notify the plate umpire when the opposing team’s pitcher has reached their pitch count limit as soon as the screen on the app turns RED. The umpire must notify the Away Team Head Coach. The Head Coach is responsible for reporting to the umpire regardless of if they have delegated the pitch count responsibilities to someone. The Away team has the right to request the pitch count for either team at any time throughout the game to ensure that the count is accurate, and rules have not been violated. [TOP] WHAT ARE MY OBLIGATIONS WHEN TRAVELING OUTSIDE OF ONTARIO OR CANADA? All Arm Care rules are mandatory when traveling outside Ontario or Canada. Games played in US or other out-of-province territories must be entered into Pitch Count with no opponent selected within 24 hours after the completion of each game. Sanctions apply for coaches who fail to report Pitch Counts, exceed thresholds, or do not follow required Days Rest periods while playing games out of province. [TOP] WHAT PENALTIES APPLY WHEN A TEAM VIOLATES THE PITCH COUNT RULES? Different penalties apply depending on which team violates the pitch count rule, whether or not the umpire was notified, as well as if and when the violation was reported. TEAM VIOLATION VIOLATION REPORTED PENALTY Home Team violates pitch count In game Forfeit + 1 game suspension for Head Coach Home Team violates pitch count Post-game No forfeit 2 game suspension for Head Coach Away Team violates pitch count after notification by Official Pitch Counter to umpire at time of infraction In game Forfeit + 1 game suspension for Head Coach Away Team violates pitch count with NO notification to umpire prior to the time of infraction In game No forfeit Head Coach is ejected + 1 game suspension for Head Coach Away team violates pitch count Post-game No forfeit 2 game suspension for Head Coach IMPORTANT NOTE ON ENFORCEMENT: The intent of these rules is to enforce the pitch count rules and not to promote forfeits. The Home Team is responsible for the pitch count and therefore the duty of responsibility falls upon them to notify the opposing team of a pitcher reaching their pitch count via the plate umpire. Regardless of whether or not, the Home Team Head Coach delegates the pitch count to someone else, they are ultimately responsible and accountable to the Pitch Count. [TOP] WHAT HAPPENS IF A TEAM IS NOT IN THE APP? If a team is not in the app, it means that their roster has not yet been submitted and the team is not permitted to play. After May 1st, any team playing without having submitted a roster shall be automatically suspended until their roster is submitted. [TOP] WHAT HAPPENS IF A PLAYER IS NOT ON THE TEAM IN THE APP? If a player is not on the team in the app, they are not permitted to pitch. Call-up players can be added only by the team they are playing on before the game starts. Once the game starts, they can’t be added to the team in the app and are therefore not allowed to pitch. [TOP] THERE'S AN ERROR ON MY ROSTER, HOW CAN I FIX IT? Pitch Count rosters are pulled directly from the BaseballShift rostering system. If there is an error on your roster in Pitch Count, it is highly likely the result of incorrect data on your BaseballShift roster. Please reach out to your Local Registrar to have your roster corrected. Some common issues include: Missing player: This is a result of the player either not being entered into your BaseballShift roster, or the player does not have a valid birthdate on the roster. Incorrect days rest applied: This is usually the result of a player having an incorrect birthdate on the roster. Caution should be taken when selecting an existing player in the rostering system to reduce duplication of players on multiple teams. Missing or wrong coach: Coaches are required to have their email address and NCCP number on their roster. Pitch Counter / Team Manager Not On App: Only staff entered with valid email addresses will be given access to the app. If you wish your Team Manager to manage Pitch Counts for you, you must select Pitch Counter in the drop down menu on the BaseballShift roster. Note: this gives any staff full access to your Pitch Count app. I'm a coach and my name appears on my team in the app, but I haven't received my login: It is likely that your email address has been entered incorrectly on your roster (or a different one than you're using). Please see below for information on submitting a trouble ticket to Pitch Count Support. [TOP] HOW DO I SUBMIT A TROUBLE TICKET FOR THE PITCH COUNT APP? If you encounter a bug, have feedback on the app, or have a question for Pitch Count Support, please email pitchcountapp@baseballontario.com BEFORE YOU EMAIL: Be sure to email from the email address associated with your Pitch Count app if possible. Always include your Team ID which can be found in the URL for your BaseballShift roster (see below). If your question is in regard to a specific game, please include the game number. If your question is in regard to a specific player or coach, please include their full name. [TOP] Last Updated: 29-July-2024 SearchMember DirectoryEventsEmail Support Copyright © 2026 New Start Mobile, LLC https://ondeck.baseballontario.com/page/784/arm-care/16475/arm-care-pitch-count-rules-faq

searchText: Rep Division Playing Rules Rep Division Playing Rules Table of Contents Skip to main content Rep Division Playing Rules – Rep Rule Book COACHING CENTRE UMPIRE ZONE FEMALE BASEBALL ARM CARE MORE SIGN IN / REGISTER Rep Division Playing Rules Updated for the 2026 Baseball Season Table of Contents RP1.1 Age and Category RP1.2 Birth Records RP1.3 Residency Rules RP1.4 Players Changing Residence RP1.5 Players Changing Teams, Releases, and Transfers RP1.6 Release Appeals RP1.7 Offenses RP1.8 Advancing to Higher Series RP1.9 Player Registration RP1.10 Team Number RP1.11 Professional Players Registration RP1.12 Eligibility RP1.13 High Performance Programs RP1.14 High Performance Provincial Championships RP1.15 Elite Baseball League of Ontario (EBLO) RP2 Rep Special Playing Rules RP2.1 Uniforms & Equipment RP2.2 Game Length RP2.3 Line-Up Rules RP2.4 Substitutions RP2.5 Pitching Rules RP2.6 Base Running Rules RP2.7 Fielding Rules RP2.8 Extra Inning Rules RP2.9 Protests RP2.10 10U/11U Playing Rules RP2.11 Eligibility RP2.12 Premier Baseball League of Ontario (PBLO) RP5 Rep Provincial Championships RP5.1 Rep Provincial Championship Procedures RP5.2 Tournament Style Rep Provincial Championships RP5.3 Home-and-Home Style Rep Provincial Championships RP5.4 Tournament Format for Rep Provincial Championships RP5.5 Determining the Home Team in Rep Provincial Championships RP6 Rep National Qualifier Tournaments RP6.1 National Qualifier Tournament Procedures RP7.1 Exhibition Games and Out-of-Province Play RP7.2 Open, Invitational, and International Tournaments RP7.3 Tie-Breaker Rules RQ1 Approved Rules of 9U Rep Appendices Appendix A - Guidelines for Affiliated Associations Appendix B - Affiliated Associations Appendix C - National Championship Player Drafts RP1.1 Age and Category To be eligible to play in a particular age category, a player must not have a birthday which makes them older than the number in the series name. ie: A player in 9U (9 and Under) must not turn 10 dururing the current calendar year. CATEGORY/SERIES AGE OF PLAYER AS OF DECEMBER 31st OF THE CALENDAR YEAR 8U Not older than 8 years of age but not younger than 6 as of December 31st 9U Not older than 9 years of age 10U Not older than 10 years of age 11U Not older than 11 years of age 12U Not older than 12 years of age 13U/13U Girls Not older than 13 years of age 14U Not older than 14 years of age 15U Not older than 15 years of age 16U/16U Girls Not older than 16 years of age 18U Not older than 18 years of age 22U Not older than 22 years of age Senior/Women’s Unlimited but not younger than 17 as of December 31st [TOP] RP1.2 Birth Records All players 21 years of age and under must produce a birth record the first time they register with OBA. Only birth certificates, passports, citizenship cards or driver’s licenses will be accepted as birth records. In the case of Affiliated Associations having their own registrar and having the approval of the OBA, it is not necessary to send birth records to the OBA Office if such birth records are provided to the Affiliated Association’s registrar and will be recorded by the registrar. For Affiliated Associations not having their own registrar or for Direct Entries, when sending birth records to the OBA Office, they must be listed, and a copy of the list included with the birth records. Upon receipt of a birth record, it will be recorded in the OBA Office. Players who cannot procure a birth record may sign a team roster upon producing a statutory declaration sworn by their parent or guardian establishing their age; the same to be accompanied by a certificate of a solicitor or notary public. [TOP] RP1.3 Residency Rules The basic premise of the Rep Division’s residence rule is that players are to play with the Local Association where they reside. Players must establish residence within the Local Association on or before June 30th of the playing year. Exception: a player, who (1) maintains Canadian citizenship, (2) has been a member of a Rep Local Association or Direct Entry for at least one year prior, (3) continues to be a member of the same Rep Local Association or Direct Entry, and (4) for any reason is living outside of the Province of Ontario, may continue to register with that Local Association or Direct Entry. Individuals may be granted permission by the Rep Registrar to participate in Rep play. Cases will be dealt with on an individual basis (i.e., exchange students). Affiliated Associations must establish territorial boundaries for Local Associations within the Affiliated Association. Affiliated Associations may also set policies applicable within their Affiliated Association. A copy of the boundaries and any related policies must be filed with Baseball Ontario. The Rep Division will consider the most recent copies filed by the Affiliated Association in any dispute. The Rep Division may, in its discretion, choose to not follow the Affiliated Association policy where it conflicts with the OBA Procedures and Playing Rules and/or the Procedures and Playing Rules of the Rep Division or the best interests of Baseball Ontario. In areas where no Local Association exists, the Affiliated Association may set policy. In the absence of such a policy, The Rep Division shall apply the following policy: The player may play for any team in the Affiliated Association without a release. In areas where there is a Local Association, but the Local Association does not have a team at the particular series, the Affiliated Association may set policy. In the absence of such a policy, The Rep Division shall apply the following policy: The player may play for any team in the Affiliated Association without a release. For the purposes of this rule, minor (e.g., 8U, 10U, 12U, 14U, 16U) is not defined as a separate series from major (e.g., 9U, 11U, 13U, 15U, 18U). If a combined minor/major program is offered by a Local Association, then the Local Association is considered to have a team at the particular series. The player is expected to return to his/her Local Association, unless a released is obtained pursuant to RP1.5. A player in the circumstances of e) or f) may play for a team in another Affiliated Association without a release where such team is the team closest to his/her place of residence. Closest team shall be interpreted as the distance from the player’s home to the relevant teams’ home baseball diamond as the crow flies. Where a Local Association has more than 1 team operating in the same series, the Local Association shall set policy and/or boundaries for such teams. The Local Association shall file a copy of such policy and/or boundaries with the Affiliated Association and Baseball Ontario. [TOP] RP1.4 Players Changing Residence Baseball Ontario recognizes that families move. As set out in RP1.3b), players must establish residence prior to June 30th of the playing season. In split family situations (i.e., the parents are divorced or separated), players 18 years of age and younger shall be deemed to reside where the parent(s) having custody rights reside. Baseball Ontario does not condone moves of convenience or falsified family splits designed to bypass the release process. Baseball Ontario, when considering the legitimacy of the family move or family split, may have regard to a number of factor(s), including, but not limited to the following: a statement from the parent(s) outlining that they actually live at the stated address; a copy of a rental agreement or proof of ownership of the residence; a driver’s license with the stated address; the name and address of the school the player attends; a separation agreement or divorce decree outlining the custody and access arrangements of the player; and/or Canada Revenue Agency Marital Status Change Form – RC 65 [TOP] RP1.5 Players Changing Teams, Releases, and Transfers Players are only allowed to be registered with one team at any time. Players registered with one team must be properly released before registering with a second team. This applies to all series. A player’s playing rights are held by: (i) the Local Association(s) where they reside as set forth in RP1.3 and RP1.4; and (ii) the Local Association where they played the previous season if the player obtained all necessary releases under this section RP1.5. At the Affiliated Association level, a player’s playing rights are held by the Affiliated Association(s) where they reside as set forth RP1.3 and RP1.4. Before a player may participate in a practice, game, tryout, evaluation, or other baseball activity with a team that does not currently hold the player’s playing rights, the player must obtain the appropriate authorization as specified below. Where a player wishes to participate in another team’s out-of-province game or tournament and is registered with the Local Association that holds their playing rights, then permission from the Local Association and the Affiliated Association are required. Where a player desires to register with a Direct Entry or Local Association that does not hold their playing rights, the player must obtain: a release from each Local Association that hold their playing rights as set out in RP1.5 b); and a release from the Affiliated Association where the player resides, if they desire to register with a Direct Entry or Local Association outside of the Affiliated Association where they reside. Note: The request for a release must be in writing and the granting or refusal of a release shall be in writing authorized by the current management of the Direct Entry, Local Association or Affiliated Association involved. The foregoing does not apply at AAAA, 22U, or Senior where no release is required, and a player may sign with a team that does not hold their playing rights without a release as long as there is no equipment, uniform or financial obligation owing to their previous team. Where a player desires to participate in a tryout, evaluation, practice, or other baseball activity with a team that does not currently hold the player’s playing rights, the player must obtain the releases required by RP1.5e). Release requests must be responded to and adjudicated by the Local Association or Affiliated Association, as the case may be, within 14 days of receipt. The Local Association or Affiliated Association may extend this due date by an additional 14 days should more information be required and must do so in writing to the person requesting the release. Should the request not be responded to within these parameters, the release will be automatically denied. Where a Local Association or Direct Entry grants a player a release, such release is valid and remains in full force and effect so long as the player continues to play for the Local Association or Direct Entry with which the player registered on the basis of having been granted such release. All releases must be in writing and cannot be conditional. Any player, for whom a Local Association is not prepared to offer a spot on one of the Local Association’s OBA teams, is deemed to be released. The Local Association is required to provide a written release upon request by the player. Any player, who has not been played by his/her team in 3 or more consecutive games, has the right to appeal to the OBA Technical Committee should the player’s team refuse to release the player. Releases shall not be valid until filed with the OBA Office. [TOP] RP1.6 Release Appeals Where a player has been denied a release under RP1.5 by a Local Association, the player has the right to appeal in writing the Local Association’s refusal to the Affiliated Association. The Affiliated Association will make a decision to grant or deny the release. The Affiliated Association’s decision is to be in writing. Either the player or the Local Association may appeal the Affiliated Association’s decision to the OBA Technical Committee. No appeal may be made until the Affiliated Association has made its decision. The appeal must include all evidence and correspondence submitted to the Local Association and/or the Affiliated Association and copies of all decisions made by the Local Association and Affiliated Association. The OBA Technical Committee will establish the time and place of the appeal hearing, which will be held within 30 days of receiving the written appeal and all documents required in RP1.6 c). The player (and/or his/her parent(s) or guardian(s)) and the Direct Entry or Local Association and/or Affiliated Association must be present in person at the hearing to provide evidence in support of their position. Failure to appear may result in a default decision. The decision of the OBA Technical Committee shall be final. [TOP] RP1.7 Offenses Where a player participates in a practice, game, tryout, evaluation, or other baseball activity with a team that does not currently hold the player’s playing rights without the authorization(s) required by RP1.5, the player is subject to suspension by OBA. Any Head Coach, Assistant Coach or manager who allows a player to participate in any practice, game, tryout, evaluation, or other baseball activity with a team that does not currently hold the player’s playing rights without the authorization(s) required by RP1.5, is subject to suspension by OBA. No player, Head Coach, Assistant Coach or manager may approach any player for whom their Local Association or Direct Entry does not hold the playing rights with the intention of securing said player for their Local Association or Direct Entry. Violation of this rule will leave the player, coach, or manager subject to suspension by OBA. Where a director, officer, employee or other member of a Local Association or Direct Entry (who is not a player, Head Coach, Assistant Coach or manager) approaches any player for whom their Local Association or Direct Entry does not hold the playing rights with the intention of securing said player for their Local Association or Direct Entry, then such violation will leave the Local Association or Direct Entry open to a fine of up to $1000. In addition to or in place of any suspension authorized by this section RP1.7, any person or organization violating any of the provisions of section RP1.7 may be subject to fine or other sanction as determined appropriate by the Technical Committee. [TOP] RP1.8 Advancing to a Higher Series A player registered with a team in any series 22U or below shall be allowed to advance to a higher series of their own Local Association without affecting their status in the series and classification of the team with which they are registered. Any player, who advances, must play with their original team when the team they advance to and the team they are registered with have games on the same date. Where the original (rostered) team is competing in a weekend tournament, the player shall not be eligible to be used as a call up to the team they are advancing to until their original (rostered) team is eliminated from competition in their tournament. (Note: Any team knowingly postponing a game so that a player may advance will be suspended from the Rep Provincial Championships.) (Local Association shall be interpreted as the Local Association with which the player is registered.) In advancing under RP1.8 a), a player may only advance as follows: A 9U (including 8U) player may only advance to 11U. A 11U (including 10U) may advance only to 13U. A 13U (including 12U) may advance only to 15U. A 15U(including 14U) may advance only to 18U. Players who turn 16 years of age in the calendar year may only advance to 22U. For the 15U and 18U National Qualifiers only, OBA teams may call players across from other teams within their Local Association at the same age category, so long as that team is not also playing in the National Qualifier (e.g., an 18U team may call a player to their National Qualifier team from their 17U team (i.e. an 18U team of all 17U aged players), or their 18U A team). For greater clarity, a player may only play for one team in any one National Qualifier tournament. Where a Local Association has more than one team at a series, players that are on the lower classified team may be “called up” to the higher classified team. Where a Local Association has both a Select Division team(s) and a Rep team(s) at a series, players from the Select Division team(s) may be called up to the Rep team(s), provided the players comply with the residency rules and are the proper age. Where a player of a younger age is rostered with a team at an older age category (e.g., A 13U aged player is rostered on a 15U roster), such player shall be considered as a player of the older series at which he/she is rostered despite being of younger age and shall not be eligible to play with the younger team. Where a team wishes to use a call up player from a team that has been eliminated from any further competition during a tournament or a Rep Provincial Championship tournament or a Rep National Qulaifier tournament being held the same weekend, must have either the pitch count records or copies of the official game sheets for all games involving the call-up player. Any player, for whom the pitching eligibility cannot be confirmed, shall not be eligible to pitch. Once discovered, the penalty for violating any provision of RP1.8 shall be forfeiture by the team to which the player advances. The Series Chair shall determine what other appropriate steps will be taken in the circumstances. The Series Chair's decision is final and is not appealable. [TOP] RP1.9 Player Registration Each year, Local Associations will register their players and teams with the OBA through an online registration platform in accordance with policies and procedures determined by the OBA. The OBA will publish such policies and procedures on its website. By virtue of submitting a roster, the Local Association accepts responsibility for the authenticity of the information contained on the roster. The process may commence by a date determined by Rep Council which shall be no later than October 1st annually. Teams must submit a preliminary roster to the OBA Office through the OBA online registration platform no later than April 28th annually or five business days prior to the team's first game, whichever is later. Any team playing without having submitted a roster after May 1st shall be automatically suspended until such time as the roster is submitted. Rosters may be edited by Local Associations until May 31st. No changes to players on a roster may be made after May 31st without the consent of the OBA Rep Commissioner. At 19U Women’s, Senior Women’s, and Senior, submitted rosters may be edited by Local Associations until June 30th. At 19U Women’s, Senior Women’s, and Senior, no changes to players on a roster may be made after June 30th without the consent of the OBA Rep Registrar. At a minimum, the information to be provided through such online registration system will include: Name Date of birth Gender Address of residence (including postal code) Telephone number, and Valid email address Affiliated Associations and the OBA may review submitted rosters to ensure compliance with requirements of player registration and coach certification. Affiliated Associations and the OBA may remove players and coaches not meeting requirements from rosters. Teams with rosters not meeting the requirements of player registration and coach certification are not eligible to participate in Rep Provincial Championships and/or Rep National Qualifier Tournaments. No player may participate in a Rep tournament or Rep Provincial Championship without their name on a team roster. If a player, who is not officially on the team roster or on the roster of a team from which the team may call up players, participates in a game, once it is discovered, the Head Coach will receive a 10-game suspension. The suspension is not appealable. Subject to Baseball Canada eligibility rules, Senior teams attending Baseball Canada Men’s Nationals may add Intercounty Baseball League players as call-ups to their roster for Nationals. [TOP] RP1.10 Team Number A team must have 9 signed players and may have a maximum of 25 players who must be signed to a team roster of the series of the team entered. At 22U and Senior, the maximum roster size is 30 players. A maximum of 25 players can appear on a line-up card for any one game. Each team must have at least 1 manager, and/or 1 or more coaches, signed to the roster clearly marked “Manager” or “Coach” and who shall be responsible for that team. [TOP] RP1.11 Professional Players Registration Any player signed by a professional baseball club of Major League Baseball (MLB) or Minor League Baseball (MiLB), who has received his/her unconditional release or has been placed on the Voluntary Retired List by said professional club before June 30th of the playing season shall become eligible for reinstatement to play in the OBA immediately when he/she returns to his/her home city or town, or to the team he/she left to turn professional or he/she may play with any other team providing he/she qualifies under the residence rule. All applications for reinstatement must be accompanied by a letter from the Office of the President of the MiLB or a copy of the release from the applicable professional organization. Any player signed by an independent professional league or club who has received his/her unconditional release or has been placed on the voluntary retired list by said independent professional club who provides a written statement advising that they no longer play in that league before June 30th of the playing season shall become eligible for reinstatement to play in the OBA immediately when he/she returns to his/her home city or town, or to the team he/she left to turn professional or he/she may play with any other team providing he/she qualifies under the residence rule. A player shall be considered to have turned professional when he/she plays his/her first game, either exhibition or league, with a professional club. The release, voluntary retirement confirmation or written statement must accompany any application for reinstatement.
